topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance meticulously stacked ungraded papers, the rhythmic *thud* of the stacks against the oak desk the only sound in her otherwise silent office. Her shoulders were tight, a constant knot of dissatisfaction settling deep in her neck. Another long week had bled into another, leaving her feeling utterly depleted. The door creaked open, and she looked up, ready to deliver a curt dismissal. Instead, standing there was Marcus, a student from her advanced literature class three years prior. He held out a small, intricately carved wooden bird, polished smooth by handling. "Professor Vance," he began, his voice surprisingly soft, "I just wanted to say... thank you. For everything." Eleanor blinked, momentarily thrown off balance. She felt a strange, unfamiliar warmth creep up her cheeks, quickly followed by a stiffening of her posture. She looked down at the bird, then back at him, her expression carefully neutral, a practiced shield against any unexpected surge of feeling.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights of the faculty lounge hummed with irritating persistence, mirroring the low-level irritation simmering beneath David Miller's skin. He was nursing a lukewarm coffee, his jaw set tight enough to crack enamel. He was trying very hard to ignore the persistent ringing in his ears that seemed to amplify every minor inconvenience. A knock sounded on his door, startling him so badly he nearly spilled his drink. It was Sarah Jenkins, a student from his introductory physics course. She entered hesitantly, clutching a small, wrapped box. "Mr. Miller," she murmured, avoiding eye contact, her gaze fixed on the floor tiles. "I found this. I thought you might like it." David frowned, the movement pulling at his brow. He took the box, the paper feeling unexpectedly delicate in his calloused hands. He felt a distinct tightening in his chest, a resistance to the sudden, unwelcome attention, and managed a short, clipped acknowledgment.

[story 3]

Rain lashed against the tall windows of the library study room, creating a dull, relentless drumming that matched the mood inside. Dr. Helen Reed was reviewing grant proposals, her focus razor-sharp, though her movements were stiff, betraying a profound sense of weariness. She hated interruptions; she preferred the solitude of complex data. Then, a hesitant presence appeared at her doorway. It was Thomas, a student she hadn't seen in nearly a decade. He offered her a small, leather-bound journal tied with a faded blue ribbon. "I know you're busy," Thomas said, his voice pitched low, "but I wanted you to have this. It reminded me of you." Helen paused, her pen hovering over the page. A slow, involuntary softening occurred around her eyes, a reaction she hadn't anticipated. She took the gift, the texture of the leather grounding her momentarily amidst the swirling discomfort of the afternoon.
